coredata.dc:title: Aliphatic amide salt, a new type of luminogen: Characterization, emission and biological applications
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qinghui
Li, Bin
Cao, Hongyan
Jiang, Xubao
Kong, Xiang Zheng
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent materials, particularly those with tunable wavelengths, are gaining considerable attention owing to their advanced applications in optoelectronic devices, fluorescent probes and sensors etc. Among all species studied, luminogens without typical chromophores have been gaining increasing attention. The related studies have been mainly focused on polymers with functional groups, such as aliphatic tertiary amine, carbonyl, ester, hydroxyl, amide, imide etc. Here we report a new type of nonconventional fluorescent emitter, fully water-soluble aliphatic amide salt. By simply blending methanol solution of ethylene diamine with that of succinic acid, the aliphatic amide salt (PA24S) was precipitated out, and identified through a series of advanced testing. Both the salt PA24S and its corresponding polyamide showed fluorescent emission as solid powders and in their solutions. The emission behavior of PA24S was studied with regard to concentration, pH, temperature and non-solvent fraction. It was concluded that the emission was owing to the formation of molecule clusters, and confirmed by UV–vis absorption, cluster size measurement and NMR test. It was also demonstrated that Cu2+ was an effective quenching agent with good selectivity. The cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that PA24S was non-toxic to HeLa cell, and suitable for cell imaging. Combined with selective quenching of Cu2+, PA24S is successfully used for Cu2+ detection and monitoring. This work provides therefore a novel type of luminogen of great potential applications in biological fields, featured by its easiness to make, high water solubility and broad emission in dilute solution and at solid state.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aliphatic amide salt
Cluster-triggered emission
Copper ion monitoring
Cell imaging
Non-toxicity

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of polyamide salt PA24S and its polymer PA24
Cluster formation and emission mechanism of PA24S
Application of PA24S for Cu2+ monitoring and cell imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photos of PA24S powder under CFM with different channel (λEx: A1, 405 nm; A2, 488 nm; A3, 561 nm; A4, Daylight); emission spectra of PA24S powder under excitation of different wavelength (B); Photos of aqueous PA24S solution (100 mg/mL) using different channel of CFM (λEx: C1, 405 nm; C2, 488 nm; C3, 561 nm; C4, Daylight); and emission spectra of PA24S solution under excitation of different wavelength (D).
Image of PA24S aqueous solutions of different concentration under UV lamp of 365 nm (A), and emission spectra of the PA24S solutions under 370 nm excitation (B).

Dependence of fluorescent emission (λEm = 452 nm) of aqueous PA24S solution on pH at different concentration under λEx = 370 nm (A), and reversibility of the emission by adding NaOH or HCl solution to that of PA24S (B, 80 mg/mL).
Particles size detected by DLS in aqueous PA24S solution at different concentration (A, pH = 6.4) and at different pH (B, 80 mg/mL; the inset is the average size versus pH).
TEM photos of the aggregates in PA24S solution at concentration of 40 mg/mL (A) and 100 mg/mL (B).
1H NMR spectra (A, in deuterated water D2O) and UV–Vis absorbance (B, in water) of PA24S solutions at different concentration.
Emission spectra of aqueous PA24S solution (20 mg/mL) with different amount of PTO in water (A) and those of the solution at 80 mg/mL at different temperature (B), all under λEx = 370 nm.
Schematic illustration of cluster structures in concentrated PA24S aqueous solution.

Fluorescence emission spectra (λEx = 370 nm) of PA24S solution (20 mg/mL) in the presence of different metal ions (50 mM, A); those of PA24S solution as function of Cu2+ concentration (B, inset is digital photos of the solutions of PA24S at 20 mg/mL, Cu2+ at 500 μM and their mixture).

Upper row: Images of CFM of HeLa cells cultured for 30 min in presence of PA24S (2.0 mg/mL) under 405 nm, 488 nm, 561 nm, bright field and their overlay; Bottom row: The same photos as those in the upper row, except an extra culturing of 20 min in the presence of Cu2+ (5 μM) following that in the presence of PA24S of 30 min.

head.ce:title: Aliphatic amide salt, a new type of luminogen: Characterization, emission and biological applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Dezhou University, Dezhou 253023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Dezhou University, Dezhou 253023, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Québec Center for Functional Materials, Department of Chemistry, Université de Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, QC J1K2R1, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Québec Center for Functional Materials, Department of Chemistry, Université de Sherbrooke, Sherbrooke, QC J1K2R1, Canada

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent materials, particularly those with tunable wavelengths, are gaining considerable attention owing to their advanced applications in optoelectronic devices, fluorescent probes and sensors etc. Among all species studied, luminogens without typical chromophores have been gaining increasing attention. The related studies have been mainly focused on polymers with functional groups, such as aliphatic tertiary amine, carbonyl, ester, hydroxyl, amide, imide etc. Here we report a new type of nonconventional fluorescent emitter, fully water-soluble aliphatic amide salt. By simply blending methanol solution of ethylene diamine with that of succinic acid, the aliphatic amide salt (PA24S) was precipitated out, and identified through a series of advanced testing. Both the salt PA24S and its corresponding polyamide showed fluorescent emission as solid powders and in their solutions. The emission behavior of PA24S was studied with regard to concentration, pH, temperature and non-solvent fraction. It was concluded that the emission was owing to the formation of molecule clusters, and confirmed by UV–vis absorption, cluster size measurement and NMR test. It was also demonstrated that Cu2+ was an effective quenching agent with good selectivity. The cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that PA24S was non-toxic to HeLa cell, and suitable for cell imaging. Combined with selective quenching of Cu2+, PA24S is successfully used for Cu2+ detection and monitoring. This work provides therefore a novel type of luminogen of great potential applications in biological fields, featured by its easiness to make, high water solubility and broad emission in dilute solution and at solid state.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their unique optoelectronic property and broad application in the fields of optoelectronic devices, drug delivery, cell imaging and biosensors, photoluminescent (PL) materials have been extensively studied [1–5]. With the development of electronic display technology, the research on new solid emitters with high efficiency has been gaining more importance owing to the high performance of the recently emerged OLEDs in comparison with traditional LEDs. Many of the achievements should be attributed at least partially to the discovery of aggregation induced emission (AIE) materials [6–12], first reported by Tang et al. [6], which has overcome the hindrance to diverse applications because of the so called aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) and made solid-state emission a reality. AIE luminogens have opened a new way to design and construct novel PL materials. Up to date, a great variety of AIE luminogens with their applications have been reported [6–20], among which are the organic molecules and polymers with highly hydrophobic conjugated structures, which are in general featured by poor water solubility and high cytotoxicity, accompanied often with serious immunogenicity and inflammation, making their biological applications troublesome [7,8,17,18]. Another widely studied group of AIE materials is often referred to as materials without conventional luminophores, which involve mainly diverse polymers known as intrinsically luminescent polymers, containing solely nonconventional chromophores, including for instances, poly(amidoamine) [18,21], poly(aminoester) [22], polyimine etc [23–25]. Different from AIE materials with highly conjugated structures, these polymers do not have classic chromophores. Incorporation of fluorescent labels on these polymers is required sometimes in order to enhance their emission performance [26–29].

Different from all the reported AIE luminogen materials, we report here a novel type of PL material, an aliphatic amide salt, which is fully water-soluble and easily prepared by simply blending ethylene diamine with succinic acid in methanol. The amide salt obtained, ethylene succinamide salt (PA24S), was fully characterized, and compared with its corresponding polymer, polyethylene succinamide (PA24). Both the salt PA24S and the polymer PA24 showed strong fluorescent emission as solid powder and in their solutions. The emission behavior of the salt PA24S in the presence of different metal ions was also tested, and an effective quenching effect by Cu2+ was observed. The cytotoxicity assay to HeLa cells showed that PA24S was non-toxic. This work provides therefore a novel type of solid luminophore with potential applications in biological and medical fields, which also has important implications on the development and understanding of the fluorescent mechanism of nonconventional PL materials.

Ethylene diamine (EDA), glycerin (PTO), methanol, ethanol, acetone, acetonitrile, dichloromethane (DCM), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N,N′-dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH), all AP grade, were purchased from Tianjin Fuyu Fine Chemicals. Butane diamine (BDA), hexamethylene diamine (HDA), oxalic acid (OA), succinic acid (SA), adipic acid (AA), formic acid, acetic acid, cadmium nitrate (Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, AP), chromic sulfate (Cr2(SO4)3·6H2O, AP), silver nitrate (AgNO3, AP), aluminum nitrate (Al(NO3)3·9H2O, AP), mercuric nitrate (Hg(NO3)2, AP), and nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AP) were all purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Copper sulfate (CuSO4·5H2O, AP), sodium chloride (NaCl, AP), calcium chloride (CaCl2, AP), lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2, AP), ferrous chloride (FeCl2, AP), ferric chloride (FeCl3, AP), potassium chloride (KCl, AP) and magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, AP) were from Tianjin Damao Chemicals. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) of Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co. Ltd, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) from Shanghai Lifei Biotechnologies, thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) from Sigma-Aldrich, deuterated trifluoroacetic acid (TFA-d) and deuterated water (D2O) of Cambridge Isotope Laboratories Inc. were all used as received. Double distilled water was laboratory-made.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of polyamide salt PA24S and its polymer PA24
ce:section.ce:para: All preparation was done under practically the same experimental conditions. Methanol (65.0 g) was added to a flask of 250 mL, installed in a water bath at 30 °C under stirring at 300 r/min, followed by simultaneous addition of SA (3.307 g, 0.028 mol) and EDA (1.683 g, 0.028 mol), both pre-dissolved in methanol (15.0 g). Soon after the start of the addition, white precipitate was observed. The reaction was continued for 4 h after completion of the addition. Solid polyamide salt, PA24S, was separated through centrifugation and filtration, rinsed with methanol and vacuum-dried. This salt, PA24S, was turned to its polyamide, i.e. poly(ethylene succinamide) (PA24), through solid phase polycondensation using a process adopted from reported studies as follow [30–32]: An amount of PA24S salt was located to an autoclave of stainless steel, and heated to 150 °C after N2 purging. The polymerization was allowed to proceed for 2 h under 0.5 MPa pressure. At end of the polymerization, the pressure was released to atmosphere, autoclave purged again by N2 to remove the water produced in the process. After cooling down to room temperature, the light-yellow solid product was ground, dissolved in formic acid, and precipitated out by adding methanol. Polyamide PA24 was obtained under form of solid powder with light yellow color.

The cytotoxicity of PA24S was evaluated by a standard MTT assay [33]. HeLa cells were inoculated into a 96-well plate, each containing approximately 5000 cells. After one day of culturing, different weight of PA24S, dissolved in DMEM, was added to the cells to make the final concentration of PA24S in the wells to be 0.00 (blank test), 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 mg/mL, respectively (6 parallel tests for each). After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, 10 μL of MTT (5.0 mg/mL in PBS, pH 7.4) was added to the cells and incubated for another 4 h. The culture medium was then removed and 100 μL of PBS was added into each well. The absorbance of each well (5 parallel tests) at 570 nm was measured by a microplate reader. The cell viability was obtained by calculation of (OD-ODb)/(ODc-ODb), with OD and ODc denoting the optical density of the cells in the presence and in the absence of the probe PA24S, and ODb denoting that of the culture medium.

For living cell imaging, HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 10% FBS in an atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, HeLa cells were incubated with PA24S (2.0 mg/mL) for 30 min, followed by cell imaging using a confocal microscope with excitation filter of 405 nm, 488 nm, 561 nm, respectively. The emission was collected at 425–475 nm (blued channel), 500–550 nm (green channel) and at 570–620 nm (red channel). HeLa cells were incubated with PA24S for 30 min, and then treated with Cu2+ (5.0 μM) for 20 min. The cells were imaged again use the same method.

Molecular weight of PA24S was estimated at 45 °C using a multi-detector gel permeation chromatography (GPC, Viscotek TDA305max, Malvern, UK), with PEG as the standard and aqueous solution of NaNO3 (0.1 M) as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 0.7 mL/min. Chemical structure analysis of PA24S by 1H and 13C NMR was done on a Digital NMR spectrometer (AVANCE III 400, Brüker). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on an RF-5301 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan) with a slit width of 5 nm for liquid samples and of 1 nm for solid samples. Part of the tests was also done using an F-7000 spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). The viscosity-average molecular weight of polyamide PA24 was determined using an Ubbleohde capillary viscometer (Φ: 0.5–0.6 mm) at 25 °C, adopted from reported studies [34,35]. The photostability of PA24S was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence intensity of the sample under continuous excitation, of which the wavelength was determined by the maximal absorbance of the sample. The following instruments were also used for different tests involved: atomic absorption spectrometer (AAS, PinAAcle-900T, Perkin Elmer, US), elemental analyzer (EA 2400II, Perkin Elmer, US), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Supra, Shimadzu, Japan), transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-1400, JEOL, Japan), scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta FEG-250, FEI, US), UV–Vis Spectrophotometer (Lambda 35, Perkin Elmer, US), confocal microscope (CFM, A1R, Nikon, Japan), dynamic light scattering nanosizer (DLS, Nano-ZS 3600, Malvern, UK), X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Focus, Brüker, Germany), Fourier transform infra-red spectrometer (FTIR, Nicolet 5700, Thermo Electron Scientific Instruments Co., US), TGA55 and DSC25 instruments (TA instruments, US).

As well known, the formation of ethylene succinamide salt, PA24S, through the reaction of EDA and SA is quite simple, owing to the interaction between the positively charged quaternary amine and the negatively charged carboxylic groups (Fig. S1). With the monomers introduced into the flask at equal moles at 30 °C, the clear monomer solution became turbid at around 3 min, indicating the formation of amide salt PA24S. A final yield of 96.0% was readily achieved within 4 h, and this yield was not increased by extending the reaction time up to 24 h. The molecular weight of the product was around 200 and remained unchanged after 4 h (Table S1). From the molecular weights of EDA (60.10) and SA (118.09), it was estimated that the salt was mainly under form of dimers. The pH of the reaction system was 6.8 at end of the reaction, and that of PA24S aqueous solution was 6.4. Based on the same reaction mechanism, nine different amide salts were prepared using combinations of different diamines (EDA, BDA and HDA) with different dicarboxylic monomers, including OA, SA and AA, respectively (Table S2). All the amide salts were easily soluble in water, glycerin and acetic acid; and insoluble in methanol, ethanol, DMSO, DMF, acetone, acetonitrile and DCM. Taking into account the structure similarity and emission intensity, the amide salt PA24S from EDA and SA was studied in the present work, as an example of the amide salts.

To identify PA24S to be the salt, elementary analysis was first carried out and the result indicated that its chemical composition was in good agreement with the theoretical values (Table S3). The salt PA24S was then subjected to NMR analysis (Fig. S2). On 1H NMR spectrum (Fig. S2A), there appeared two resonance peaks, one at 2.09 ppm, assigned to H atoms of ethylene between the two carbonyls from SA; and the other appeared at 3.04 ppm, assigned to those between the two amides originated from EDA. The integral area ratio was 1:1 as expected. The third peak at 4.79 ppm was owing to H atoms of H2O in the solvent (D2O) [36]. As to the 13C NMR spectrum (Fig. S2B), three peaks were observed. The one at 182.19 ppm was ascribed to C atoms of the carbonyl groups, while the two at 33.70 and 36.56 ppm were assigned to the C atoms of ethylene between the two carbonyls and those between the two amides. This was further confirmed by 1H-13C HSQC spectra (Fig. S2C).

In order to confirm that this PA24S was indeed the salt, the corresponding polyamide, i.e. PA24, was prepared through solid phase polycondensation [31], with a yield of 75%. The viscosity-average molecular weight was of 1700. Different from PA24S, PA24 was not soluble in water but in strong protic solvents (formic acid, dichloro- and trifluoro-acetic acid) [31]. PA24 and PA24S were analyzed by FTIR and their spectra compared along with the monomers (Fig. S3); they were also subjected to NMR analysis using TFA-d as the solvent because PA24 was not soluble in water (Fig. S4). XRD diffraction (Fig. S5) and thermal analysis (TGA & DSC, Fig. S6) were also carried out for PA24 and PA24S, and the results compared. They were also examined under SEM (Fig. S7). In addition, XPS analysis was also conducted for PA24S and PA24, along with the diacid monomer SA (Fig. S8). The results (Table S4) indicated that, the binding energy of the C atoms in carboxylic group of SA was 288.7 eV; that of the C atoms in carbonyl group of PA24 (corresponding to the C atoms in carboxyl group in SA) was 288.6 eV, slightly different; while the same C atoms in PA24S (under form of anionic carboxylic anion) were shown to have a binding energy of 288.0 eV, largely different from those in SA monomer and polyamide PA24. This was a clear signal that the carboxyl group in PA24S was indeed present under form of amide salt. And in the salt, the carboxylic group under anionic form was associated with the quaternary ammonium (Fig. S1). The electrons of the two oxygen atoms on the anionic carboxylic group were equalized because of the delocalization of their electrons. This was also affirmed by the presence of only one single and large peak of O 1s (Fig. S8D, Table S4). All these tests demonstrated that PA24S was indeed an amide salt, of which the properties were significantly different from the polymer PA24. In addition, PA24S was also prepared in different solvents, including water, DMSO, DMF and acetonitrile. All products were subjected to FTIR analysis, which showed exactly the same spectra as that of PA24S prepared in methanol (Fig. S9), indicating that exactly the same aliphatic amide salt was formed regardless of the solvent used.

It is to point out that all the afore-mentioned nine amide salts showed fluorescent emission when excited at 370 nm (Fig. S10), though the excitation wavelength of their maximal emission was different (Table S5). Under irradiation of UV lamp (365 nm), PA24S powder showed bright blue emission (Fig. S11). Under confocal microscope at different channel, the powder displayed blue, green and red colors (Fig. 1 , photos A1, A2, A3). With excitation wavelength (λEx) changed from 240 nm to 560 nm, the maximal emission (λEm) red-shifted from 320 nm to 580 nm, as shown in Fig. 1B. For aqueous solution of PA24S (100 mg/mL), the same behavior was observed: under confocal microscope at different channel, the solution displayed also different color as the powder (Fig. 1, photos C1, C2, C3); and with λEx increased from 240 nm to 540 nm, the maximal λEm red-shifted from 450 nm to 575 nm. The emission intensity increased first, showed a maximum at λEx around 370 nm, and quickly attenuated with longer λEx. All these observations demonstrate that PA24S emission is excitation dependent, an important feature observed only for limited luminogens.

Quantum yield of PA24S powder at 420 nm emission (λEx = 340 nm) was determined to be 8%, fluorescence lifetime was 3.67 ns (Fig. S11). For its solution (100 mg/mL), the quantum yield under excitation at 370 nm was about 7.5%, in good agreement with the powder. Light stability was also measured for the powder, which showed a decrease below 2% in emission intensity after 60 min of irradiation under light of 340 nm (Fig. S11), indicating that PA24S was of good light stability in comparison with dye molecules [37].

Following these interesting behaviors, aqueous PA24S solution of different concentration was prepared, and their emission tested. Under UV lamp (365 nm), all the solutions showed blue emission, which was significantly intensified with PA24S concentration (Fig. 2 A). Under 370 nm light excitation, the emission spectra of these solutions demonstrated clearly that the emission was concentration dependent: emission was detected only when concentration reached 1.0 mg/mL; below this concentration no emission was detected; and above 1.0 mg/mL, emission intensity started to increase significantly with the increase in concentration. While the emission intensity was closely concentration dependent, the maximal λEm appeared always at 452 nm, independent of the concentration (Fig. 2B). In contrast to the ACQ effect commonly observed in conventional organic luminogens [1,5–7], the emission behavior of PA24S was very similar to the AIE effect [6,19] and the CTE effect, all proposed by Tang and his colleagues [38–40]. PA24S emission is therefore strongly believed to be caused by aggregation or cluster formation of PA24S molecules.

In consideration of the nature of PA24S, it is easy to conceive that pH of its solution must have impact on its emission. The emission of PA24S solution at different pH was therefore studied. Results given in Fig. 3 A indicated that, while the emission intensity was quite constant in the pH range from 2 to 7, the intensity sharply decreased once out of the range, in particular at pH > 7. A decrease of about 75% in the intensity was observed with pH increased from 7 to 13. This pH dependence of emission was observed regardless of the concentration as long as the concentration was high enough (≥1.0 mg/mL) that emission was detectable. It is to note that this decrease in emission intensity was well reversible, i.e. the maximal emission was recovered by changing the pH back to its initial value, either by adding NaOH to the solution at low pH, or by adding HCl to the solution at high pH (Fig. 3B). These observations can be also understood based on the CTE mechanism, since the dimer salt of PA24S was formed at neutral pH and they became emissive at a given concentration (1.0 mg/mL) or higher because of clusters started to form at this concentration. With pH driven to a too high zone, the quaternary ammonium salt would disappear; the cluster or aggregation would therefore dismantle, leading to the decrease in emission intensity, and even the emission quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cluster formation and emission mechanism of PA24S
ce:section.ce:para: As described above, the emission of PA24S was believed to be owing to aggregation or formation of clusters due to interaction of their functional groups. To have support for this statement, aqueous solutions of PA24S of different concentration and at different pH were subjected to DLS test. The results given in Fig. 4 A show that the PA24S solution was not a veritable solution by conventional definition, “aggregates” or “clusters” were present because “particles” were effectively detected with given size, and this size of the particles was also increasing with PA24S concentration. The detected average size of the particles was 0.6 nm at concentration of 10 mg/mL, it increased to 120 nm at 40 mg/mL, and further to 300 nm at 80 mg/mL. This increase in size of the particles was well synchronized with the increase in emission intensity (Fig. 2B). The fact that particles were not detected at a lower concentration, where PA24S became emissive in its solution (1.0 mg/mL for instance), may be simply because the size of the clusters was beyond the detectable size limit of the instrument (0.3 nm for Nano-ZS 3600, Malvern). The formation of PA24S clusters and their size evolution as function of pH was also tested by DLS for the sample with concentration of 80 mg/mL (Fig. 4B). The results revealed that, while the size of the clusters was the largest with pH between 3.6 and 6.4, it was decreased once the pH was driven either to a lower or to a higher value, in good agreement with the emission dependence on pH displayed in Fig. 3.

Following the DLS results, PA24S solutions of different concentration were subjected to TEM observation. No any form of particles was observed in the sample at 10 mg/mL concentration. However, particles-like aggregates were indeed observed at higher concentration. The size of the aggregates was different. For the sample at 40 mg/mL (Fig. 5 A), the size of the largest particles was about 100 nm, and the smallest was far below 100 nm. The presence of these particles became very distinct for the sample at 100 mg/mL concentration (Fig. 5B). And their size was significantly larger than that at 40 mg/mL (Fig. 5A). It is to point out that one cannot exclude that these observed aggregates were formed at water evaporation knowing that TEM samples were prepared by placing a droplet of the solution on TEM grid and the observation was done after drying up the grid. Nevertheless, taking into accounts the results from DLS, TEM observation provided a supplementary support for the formation of PA24S clusters or aggregates.

In NMR analysis, the chemical shift of an atom is closely related to its environment, not only its adjacent chemical groups in the molecule concerned, but also the interaction between the atom and the neighboring molecules including the solvent. In general, the chemical shift of a proton will move to higher field when its surrounding electron density is increased, either by attaching an electron donating substituent to the molecule that the proton belongs to or by electron delocalization from any surrounding molecules. This has been used for studies of micelle formation and transformation [41–45]. The existing state of PA24S molecules was therefore investigated by NMR for PA24S solutions of different concentration, 1H NMR spectra are given in Fig. 6 A. The peaks at right side of 2.5 ppm are assigned to the protons on ethylene from SA, and those in between 3.0 and 3.5 ppm assigned to those of EDA (Fig. S2). Both peaks displayed remarkable shift towards higher field (lower chemical shift) with increased PA24S concentration. This was a solid support that the electron density around the ethylene protons was increasing with increased PA24S concentration, indicating an intermolecular arrangement which made the molecules to form cluster. The further peaks shift to higher field (lower ppm) with further increased concentration was the indication of an increase in the size of the cluster. All these were in good agreement with the results from DLS and TEM, and also in accordance with the fluorescence enhancement with the increase in PA24S concentration (Fig. 2B). In addition, the size of the cluster was determined by DLS at different time of up to 48 h (Fig. S12), which confirmed that the cluster size was quite stable, the average size was around 420 nm with the same profile of the distribution.

Under irradiation, electrons on the out-layer of an atom of a material absorb a photon and excited to a higher energy orbital. Emission occurs when these excited electrons return back to their initial state by release of the absorbed energy through light radiation. Therefore, UV–vis absorbance is an effective means to explore the mechanism of emission [12,46]. UV–vis absorbance was therefore conducted for PA24S aqueous solution of different concentrations. The results in Fig. 6B demonstrated clearly that all the solutions had UV–vis absorbance and the maximal wavelength red-shifted with increased PA24S concentration. It is particularly interesting that a new absorbance appeared at 370 nm when PA24S concentration reached 40 mg/mL and thereafter. In addition, the intensity of this absorbance was increasing with concentration, suggesting that a new type of matter for UV–vis absorption was created. It is well understood that there were no any other compounds but PA24S, the only difference between these aqueous solutions was their concentration, the new absorption must arise from the same PA24S. It is easy to conceive that the increase in concentration made PA24S molecules more crowded, and clusters may easily form by building up H bonding between –NH3 + and –COO−. With the cluster formation, electron holes would be rearranged with electrons delocalization in PA24S. This explains well not only the evolution of UV–vis absorbance with PA24S concentration (Fig. 6B), but also the NMR results (Fig. 6A) and the emission behavior (Fig. 2). This is also in good agreement with the results from DLS and TEM, supporting the conjecture of cluster formation for PA24S at certain concentration.

To provide a simple and supplementary support to the cluster formation, fluorescence emission was also tested for the two monomers and their mixture in water, all at equal concentration of 40 mg/mL, and compared with that of PA24S. The results demonstrated clearly that, in contrast to PA24S, there was practically no detectable emission for the aqueous solutions of the monomers and their mixture (Fig. S13). This enabled one to conclude that PA24S was existed in a state different from individual EDA and SA molecules.

A typical characteristic of CTE materials is that their emission is usually closely dependent on motion ability of their molecules, which is adjustable by viscosity of their solution as well as the presence of non-solvent [1,12,38–40,46–48]. Glycerol (PTO, viscosity = 945 mPa·s at 25 ℃) is a solvent of PA24S and its viscosity is much higher than water. By adding PTO to aqueous solution of PA24S and monitoring the emission behavior, it was seen that emission intensity was indeed increased with the increase in PTO (Fig. 7 A). In fact, the same increase was also observed with addition of non-solvent methanol or ethanol to PA24S aqueous solution (Fig. S14), suggesting that PA24S emission was type of CTE.

Another characteristic of CTE materials is that their emission is usually increasing with the decrease in temperature [1,17,40,48–50]. The results, obtained with temperature from 2 °C to 75 °C, are shown in Fig. 7B, which revealed that the emission intensity was decreasing gradually with increased temperature. This is because that the discrete clusters are generally prone to structural dissociation at elevated temperatures. It was also possible that this decrease in intensity was owing to an increase in non-irradiative migration by increasing temperature. The non-irradiative channel is thermally activated at low level at low temperature; the excited electrons can emit photons through radiation. With temperature increase, the non-irradiative channels become more activated thermally [48], the emission intensity is therefore reduced. All these tests indicated that the emission behaviors of PA24S were in agreement with typical CTE materials.

It is well known that UV absorbance is a reflection of the energy absorbed by excited electrons transition to a higher state. In the wavelength zone of 200–400 nm, there are in general two different types of electron transitions: one is the π → π* transition of high energy occurring between double bonds, the related absorbance peak usually shifts towards short wavelength with decreased polarity of the solvent used; another is n → π* transition of low energy occurring in compounds containing hybrid atoms (O, N and S etc). The absorbance peak owing to this transition usually shifts towards long wavelength with decreased polarity of the solvent used [51]. Based on these principles, PA24S solutions (40 mg/mL) in binary mixtures of water-methanol and water-ethanol were prepared and their UV absorbance measured. The results showed that, with decreasing polarity of the solvent mixture by increasing its alcohol content, UV absorbance of PA24S solution shifted to longer wavelength (Fig. S15). This enabled one to conclude that UV absorbance and therefore the fluorescence emission in PA24S was likely owing to the electron transition of the type n → π*. Based on PA24S structure, it is suggested that this transition may occur between O atoms and their neighboring carbonyl double bonds, or less likely between N atoms to its surrounding carbonyl double bonds once clusters were formed.

As mentioned above, PA24S emission was excitation dependent (Fig. 1), a behavior often observed in fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. These materials are featured by their possession of C atoms with hybrid orbital of sp3 or sp2, and chemical groups with O atoms [52]. Size effect has been the widely accepted theory for excitation dependent emission [53–56], i.e. the energy gaps are determined by the size of the localized sp2 clusters and their size distribution, and emission is determined by this band gap [57]. The smaller is the size, the wider is the energy gap. The fact that PA24S emission was excitation dependent was exactly because clusters of different size were formed.

Based on all above tests and analyses, it is suggested that PA24S did not emit or show very low emission intensity at low concentration because their molecules were dissolved individually and largely distanced each other. With concentration increase, the molecules became much closer, the clusters were formed through their terminal ionic groups (–NH3
+ and –COO−) and H bonding, as illustrated by Fig. 8 . The formation of these clusters made the interaction easier for the electrons on O and N atoms and on the carbonyl double bonds, promoting a rearrangement of the electron holes, amplifying the electron conjugation and delocalization, and leading to a hyper-conjugation structure in the clusters, which endowed PA24S with fluorescent emission. Compared to free molecules, the inter- and intra-molecular mobility of PA24S molecules in the clusters were more restricted, and their emission became more significant, or concentration related. The number and size of the clusters were concentration dependent, which made their emission excitation dependent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PA24S for Cu2+ monitoring and cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the capability of PA24S as sensor to detect metal ions, its selectivity was evaluated by screening a variety of metal ions at a concentration of 50 mM (Fig. 9 A). The fluorescent emission of PA24S (20 mg/mL) was completely quenched by copper ions, while none or a very slight change for the emission was observed for other tested ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Al3+, Fe3+ and Cr3+). This demonstrates that PA24S can be used for sensitive detection of Cu2+, which renders PA24S great potential for valuable applications, in particular in biological field. In accordance, the UV absorbance of PA24S solution, observed at 370 nm (Fig. 6), completely disappeared when Cu2+ (50 mM) was present (Fig. S16). PA24S molecules are typically electron-rich substrates that can easily bind to electron-deficient ions via donor–acceptor interactions, quenching luminescence from electron-rich substrate by electron or charge transfer. However, this interaction does not occur to any electron-deficient ions, where comes the selectivity of a fluorescent material towards a specific ion. As to the present interaction between Cu2+ (electron-deficient) and PA24S (electron-rich), it is reported that Cu2+ is able to combine with amine moieties in a fluorescent material to form a copper amine complex, leading to an internal filtering effect and quenching the fluorescence [58]. It is well known that Cu2+ is of great importance for human life, and plays important roles in various physiological processes and metabolism for all organisms, such as cellular respiration, bone formation, and tissue development [59]. However, free Cu2+ generates reactive oxygen species and causes apoptosis in cells. It is also believed that accumulation of free Cu2+ in human body is the possible cause for kidney and liver damage and neurodegenerative diseases etc [3,60]. Accurate detection of Cu2+ in biological systems and their environmental monitoring is therefore of great importance. In addition, when Cu2+ was introduced into PA24S solution, the solution with light green color at low Cu2+ concentration turned slightly purple and became dark purpled when Cu2+ concentration reached 500 μM (Fig. 9B, inset). Fluorescence intensity of PA24S solution was measured as function of Cu2+ concentration. The results indicated that the emission intensity was regularly and gradually decreasing with the increase in Cu2+ concentration (Fig. 9B). A good linearity was obtained through data fitting, which gave I/I0 = 1.00215–0.05184[Cu2+] with a correlation coefficient of 0.9957 for Cu2+ concentration inferior to 45 μM (Fig. S17). These results demonstrate that PA24S can be used for Cu2+ monitoring.

The cytotoxicity of PA24S was evaluated by standard MTT assay as described in experimental part [33]. The results showed clearly that, after culturing of HeLa cells for 24 h in the presence of PA24S (0.01–2.0 mg/mL), the cell viability was always above 89% (Fig. S18), a significantly low cytotoxicity [61], suggesting that PA24S was of good biocompatibility and may be used for cellular imaging.

Based on the results, HeLa cells were cultured in the presence of PA24S (2.0 mg/mL) at 37 °C, analyzed under CFM with λEx of 405 nm, 488 nm and 561 nm. The results given in Fig. 10 (upper row) demonstrate that the cells did not show perceptible changes in their shape and morphology after the incubation, and displayed strong blue, green, and red fluorescence, respectively; whereas under sunlight, the bright-field photo did not show any color; and the overlap of the images indicated the green fluorescence was of the highest intensity. This result of multicolor expression means that PA24S is of potential use for cell co-localization, organelle mobility of cellular or tissue, and monitoring of biochemical process.

Taking into accounts the good fluorescence stability, biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and the high sensitivity towards Cu2+, the culturing of HeLa cells with PA24S was extended by 20 min in the presence of Cu2+ (5 μM), and the fluorescence completely disappeared after this supplementary culturing, regardless of the excitation light (Fig. 10, bottom row). It is widely accepted that the concentration of intracellular Cu2+ is around 10 μM [3], double of the Cu2+ concentration in this test (5 μM), which suggests strongly that PA24S is of great potential as a sensitive fluorescent probe for sensitive detection of Cu2+ under physiological conditions.

By blending EDA with SA in methanol, the aliphatic amide salt PA24S, i.e. ethylene succinamide salt, precipitated out. Similar amide salts were prepared with diamines and diacids of different carbon number, and they were all fluorescent with quantum yield of about 8%. This study, focusing on the amide salt PA24S, revealed that this amide salt was fluorescent in their solid powder and in their aqueous solution, a novel type of non-conventional luminogen. The salt formation was confirmed by multi-characterizations, including elementary analysis, NMR, FTIR, XRD, TGA, DSC and XPS, and its properties compared with the polyamide prepared from the same monomers. Fluorescent emission of PA24S was excitation dependent, with the emission covering a wide range from UV to visible zone under λEx varied from 240 nm to 540 nm. The emission was also concentration dependent: no emission was detected below 1.0 mg/mL, and emission intensity was in constant increase distinctly with the increase in concentration, while the maximal λEm was unchanged, a typical feature of CTE fluorescent materials. Cluster formation was therefore studied using various tests, including emission variation with pH, DLS, TEM and NMR. The results indicated that clusters of PA24S were formed and their size was dependent on concentration. The CTE mechanism was also confirmed by UV–vis absorbance, and by emission dependence on non-solvent and viscosity of the solution and on temperature. Type of the electron transition in the emission process was also elucidated by measuring UV–vis absorbance of PA24S in solvent mixture of different polarity. Among fourteen metal ions tested, only Cu2+ was shown to quench PA24S emission with a good linearity between its concentration and the emission intensity. Cytotoxicity evaluation of PA24S against HeLa cells revealed PA24S was no toxic, of good biocompatibility and was effectively used for cellular imaging and Cu2+ monitoring. Compared with all reported luminogens up to date, this amide salt is facile to prepare with low costing, of high photostability, excellent water solubility, low cytotoxicity, and easy to scale up production. All these features endow the amide salt with great potential as a sensitive fluorescent probe for cell imaging and sensitive detection of Cu2+ under physiological conditions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ZR2018BB049, ZR2018MB021, ZR2019MB031), and by Science & Technology Development Plan (2017GGX202009) of Shandong Province, China.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2020.124182.

ce:section.ce:para:
